title: To amend the Aquifer Recharge Flexibility Act to clarify a provision relating to conveyances for aquifer recharge purposes.
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier to use underground water storage projects on federal land. People who already have permission to transport water across government land can now also use that same permission to store water underground in natural underground areas called aquifers. They can do this for themselves or help states, tribes, and public groups do it too. They just need to tell the government 30 days ahead of time and do not have to pay extra fees unless they are making money from it.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Conveyance for Aquifer Recharge Purposes
sections.text: This section makes changes to an existing law called the Aquifer Recharge Flexibility Act. An aquifer is an underground layer of rock or soil that holds water. Aquifer recharge means adding water to these underground water sources. The changes allow people who already have permission to use federal land for things like pipelines or canals to also use that same permission for aquifer recharge projects. They can work with states, local governments, tribal governments, or public organizations to do this. They do not need to get additional permission from the federal government. However, they must give the Bureau of Land Management at least thirty days notice before starting. The notice must explain who will be doing the aquifer recharge work, which existing permission they plan to use, what exactly they plan to do, and provide a copy of their agreement with the government organization they are working with. The section also makes clear that this law does not excuse anyone from following other federal laws and policies. It also does not give anyone permission to build new infrastructure or change existing infrastructure. Finally, the section makes some small word changes to make the law clearer.
